speaker: interviewer
text: Six. Mr. Malkin, what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the gloves that were casting in-- in-- in America here. Forgotten the name of the founder, but it's a very famous one. This is the gloves that with them, I captured Eichmann. The real gloves, I gave it to [INAUDIBLE], the other prosecutor in Manhattan. And he represented them to the Jewish Holocaust-- how do you say-- Heritage Museum that's going to be built in the future here in New York. But they have the gloves, the real gloves.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have the actual gloves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And over there, they have at the museum there. I think, the manager is [INAUDIBLE]. Name is [INAUDIBLE]. And they will have it. They have the actual.

speaker: interviewer
text: To establish, when you actually caught Eichmann, how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About around 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 30 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your book, Mr. Malkin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was written here and it was published here in-- in 1990. You see, co-writing with Harry Stein, and it was translated to many languages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a pity it was not distributed enough here, but still, that's the book. It's going to be a movie in the future with Robert Duvall. He's going to portray Eichmann. I don't know who's going to do me. That's not important, but that-- that's the book. And here, I wrote most I could the memories, not all of it. But that I think that were most essential memories that I had were important to tell the people about the story of the capture of Eichmann. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Malkin, this book is the book that you actually did your paintings in, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I sat with him-- you know, everybody had this book, I studied about before I-- I went to Argentina to study what is Argentina, what's in South America? And that was very important for me to study the area where I'm going to act, you know, the-- the capture of Eichmann. And this was exactly in 1960. As you see, it's written on the book. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I was sitting with him, suddenly-- or before even-- I-- I liked to paint. And that was one of my doing at the time. I started to paint in-- in the book. And I had all the painting because I had, you know, had to disguise him, forging papers. So we had all this material. And I had-- and the book was near me, and I said, if something happen, I can always close the book and nobody will see. Not my team, and not the others. And I start to paint, and I never knew it will be important. Only 17 years later when my mother died, I discovered the book. And suddenly the people from the museum, the Jewish-- the museum in Israel, in Jerusalem-- not Jewish Museum. Museum in Jerusalem found it very important to show it. And that became-- the book became famous. They showed it in many places, in many museums. And in it, I made the paintings.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will see those.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that we'll see maybe later. OK. OK. You see from your left, you see the man. That's Eichmann in-- in uniform, and I described him as much as I could with my paintings I have on the-- on the pages of the book, the handbook of South America.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other side, you see I just put my memories. I didn't want that there will be a clean memory. So I disguised them with the painting. And I thought many times what will happen if somebody will see them? I say to hell with it. If they capture Eichmann, they can also capture the book. Nothing will happen. So I disguise them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, they said that the right painting you see with the writing is a very new way of of doing paintings, that it was done by one of very famous German, other people that have done it. And it came out that I was one of the first to start painting on pages and with-- and words. And I write, words and colors make you painting and not only paint. So that we'll see there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, this is an example of two houses that I painted there, that we rented, and I just put them on paintings through my memory because we didn't-- at the time, we hadn't been so-- nobody thought about taking pictures or recording Eichmann. Nobody thought about media. They thought how to make the job. But me, as a painter, that I never thought about it, and I painted from both sides of the page. I never thought that they would be important. And suddenly it came like, you know, as memories. It is a memories in painting. So this is the two houses I made at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see here this is the other two pages. They were taken out of the book by the-- by the-- the museum in Jerusalem, and I didn't want to put them back. And this-- on the right side, you'll see the concentrated people of one faith. You see them as a concentrate together, as somebody forced them to be together as it happened like concentrate and they are the people of one faith that I thought about these people who survived or went to the camps. And the other side of there, you see a real clean page that behind it you see some colors that was painted on other side. I didn't ever thought I'm going to show them, you know, on-- in the future. So I-- I painted them on both sides. So the painting is not close to the-- to the wall. It's always, you know, on the side that you can see the both sides of the page. OK? What did you use to paint?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used my painting was-- you know, because I disguised them-- I took a lot of-- how do you say-- acrylic paints, water paints-- how do you call it? What women take, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Makeup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Makeup paints. And all of it, I mixed it until it came out to my-- I have a lot of time-- that will come out that I feel that it-- it matches the-- the page. What happened to me suddenly, I looked at the page, small pages at night, and suddenly I forgotten they are small because they protected me as a painting. But later in the book, when I saw it, suddenly I remembered, oh, I did it in a book.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when you look at it a long time, you see-- even it small-- when you see it-- see it big, you don't feel it was on a small page. And I don't know how I did it. I can't do it again. All of these were done at-- during--

speaker: interviewee
text: During these four months-- four months of the-- in [INAUDIBLE], before and when I was with him and later, OK? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see from the left, let's say the left, you see the small Jesus Christ on an altar like this, and I wrote a few things. It's written there, I think, that I've seen in many places a small Jesus Christ. And I see-- I see him always on the top, on the top of the building, you know, on the roof. I said, in the cold in the winter, he always stands there. And I say, how is it happen-- how did it happen that a small Jew, one Jew, top so high?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I looked at him well, I saw he was nailed in the hands and the feet. Even he want to go down, he couldn't. So it looks like somebody-- even he looks high, there's a good reason he has Jesus Christ, that he-- that after all he was Jewish that he couldn't go down. And that's because I was so many times in my life in the church, how do you say, somehow, I thought about him as a Jew that he-- you know, that he became Jesus Christ. I don't know if he himself even knew it. [LAUGHS] But he, in a way, touched my feeling. And I read a-- a lot about Jesus Christ, about three, four books. And what I'm showing here, the small Jesus Christ on the roof, on the altar, standing. And that's what I painted at that time. And the right side, I'm telling about were all the stories that I talked with Eichmann all these. And my coming back to stories, I say oh, the head couldn't carry himself even hundreds of hands. It's very symbolic. Couldn't carry and it's like the stone, all these names and all these graves will stand still as-- as this marble, or this kind of head, and the hands of marble that have to support him. Nobody-- how do you say-- it's just so heavy that nobody can support all these stories. Only so many hands. It's only a very symbolic. It's so-- after hearing him telling me the stories and I, in my mind, translating them. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see another two pages. On the right is only exactly like it was. I didn't paint anything on it. And one of the pages of the book, don't know the number. Maybe if you look, you see the number there.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other side, you see-- well, you see this is the fear of the mother and the child. It's very symbolic, but you see it through her face, the fear in her eyes. And you see somebody in this small round like-- like the moon, there's somebody watching her. And if you see-- if you go near to it, you see it's a face in this small face, this small-- and that symbolizes the mother and the child and the fear that he exposed to me during our talking. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] OK, and here you see Jesus Christ that I have seen in many times, in many churches when I was there just spending my time looking in the stained glass and all this. I did it with my paints that I have it with a kitchen knife, I remember. All was done with a knife, and it gave me the feeling that there's an expression of Jesus Christ. On the left side is the three children of my sister that they look like clowns, small clowns. The dresses are like clowns. And you see the shoes. And they're really-- when I looked at them in the paint and at that time, the pictures like drawing, like a painting. And the children being dressed you know, like long trousers, not suited, you know, not like today, modern dresses. That thing, you know, just whatever you have, you put it on them. And it just symbolizes like small three clowns that they can't tell their story anymore. And they died with my sister in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- one was, I think, was called [? Ruvan ?] and one was Michael and one was Joshua all somehow from the Bible. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, there you see, as I remember from the pictures and from as I remembered my-- my sister that I painted her from the memory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I will never forget her face because when I was in my mother's home, I tried not to forget her ever. And I said whatever will happened, I can even paint her now easily from my memory. And that's my sister, Fruma, that I dedicated her my book because she was my sister, my mother, and my memories and-- and the-- the memories that were all mine, the survivors of my family, and all the survivors that they suffered from from the-- from the Nazis. And I can't-- when I ever see her picture, I couldn't understand how-- how she could stay with all this torturing and separating from her three children and go to the gas chamber and then take the children away. It was terrible, and I still can't understand them. Will never understand whatever will happen, how it happened. And nobody can explain it, and even the German could never explain this thing. And I don't think there's anybody alive who can explain how it would-- how this-- it happened. And even after the life of all the survivors, if they were all dead, this would stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you paint this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you paint this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I paint it exactly in 1960, in the time of the-- after I saw the pictures in my home when my mother was there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These are my parents. I found this picture, and I think the only picture I have. And that's my mother on the right and my father on the left. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name again was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva and in Hebrew, we said Hava. And here, David is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before the death of my father. Was about very long ago. I can't give you exact-- exact-- this is very long ago. My father was alive, and he died when I was-- OK. Well, this is one of my drawings in the-- in the small book, the red book. And this is Eichmann as I've seen him with his glasses. That's the way he looked to me. And I put him on the map of Argentina. You can see that's one of the drawings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It looks like once upon a time that was me. I was young, about 30 years old when I went and-- went to capture of Eichmann. And that is one of my-- of my pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way I looked, and it was a picture in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, that was taken 1960.
